title: Optimization Algorithm Use Case Matrix
description: A comprehensive comparison table showing the suitability of 8 optimization algorithms across 12 distinct problem spaces based on landscape, cost, and dimensionality.

problem_spaces.label: Continuous, differentiable
problem_spaces.description: Smooth objective functions where exact gradients are efficiently computable.

problem_spaces.label: Expensive black-box
problem_spaces.description: Functions with no known analytical form that take minutes to days to evaluate.

problem_spaces.label: Combinatorial / discrete
problem_spaces.description: Search spaces defined by discrete structures like graphs, permutations, or boolean strings.

problem_spaces.label: Mixed-integer
problem_spaces.description: Problems containing both continuous variables and discrete or integer variables.

problem_spaces.label: Noisy objectives
problem_spaces.description: Objective functions that return stochastic or noisy outputs for the same input.

problem_spaces.label: High-dimensional black-box
problem_spaces.description: Gradient-free problems with hundreds or thousands of parameters.

problem_spaces.label: Low-dimensional, cheap
problem_spaces.description: Continuous black-box problems with fewer than 10 parameters and sub-second evaluation times.

problem_spaces.label: Highly multimodal
problem_spaces.description: Rugged fitness landscapes with many local optima requiring global exploration.

problem_spaces.label: Convex and smooth
problem_spaces.description: Simple bowl-shaped landscapes where local optima are guaranteed to be global optima.

problem_spaces.label: Highly parallelizable
problem_spaces.description: Scenarios where multiple function evaluations can be executed concurrently on clusters.

problem_spaces.label: Medium-dimensional black-box
problem_spaces.description: Continuous black-box functions with 10 to 100 parameters.

problem_spaces.label: Nonlinear constraints
problem_spaces.description: Search spaces restricted by complex, non-linear inequality or equality constraints.
